data.title: PayPay Targets US IPO On Accelerating Growth, High Profitability
data.description:
PayPay Is Growing Rapidly And Profitably

PayPay Corporation (PAYP) has filed to raise growth capital in a U.S. IPO, per an F-1 regulatory statement.

The firm provides a platform for payment and other digital banking services to users in Japan.

PAYP is growing impressively and profitably as it benefits from its major relationship with SoftBank and its ecosystem of distribution partners.

I’ll provide an update when we get more pricing and valuation details.

What Does PayPay Do?

PayPay was launched in 2018 to provide a scalable and integrated digital finance app and system for Japanese users.

The company's service offerings include:

  • Electronic payments

  • Banking services

  • Consumer loans

  • Investment services

  • Credit cards

  • Merchant terminals

  • Merchant financing

  • Marketing solutions

  • Rewards

Approximately 60% of the firm’s revenue came from payments in the most recent fiscal year, followed by Interest Income and Financial Services, as the pie chart shows below:

As of December 31, 2025, the company claimed 72 million registered users out of a total number of smartphone users of 96 million in Japan.

The firm is headed by President and CEO Mr. Ichiro Nakayama, who has been with the firm or its predecessor since 2018. He was previously the President of IDC Frontier and EVP of Ikyu Corporation.

What Is PayPay’s Market?

The digital banking market in Japan was estimated at $3.2 billion in 2024 and is expected to exceed $7.5 billion by 2035, according to a 2026 market research report by Market Research Future.

If accomplished, this growth would represent a CAGR of 8.0% from 2025 to 2035, a moderate rate of growth for this predominantly cash-oriented society.

Mobile banking adoption is increasing, driven by younger demographic users and improving technological offerings by vendors.

These vendors are also working to integrate AI and automation capabilities to improve customer service, reduce fraud and increase operational efficiencies in the process.

The chart below shows the historical and projected future growth trajectory of the Japanese digital banking market through 2035:

The company is also a major player in the payments industry, which is also a very large market that is growing.

Competitors to the company include financial services firms in the banking, brokerage, credit card, payment processing, merchant services and lending industries.

The firm’s strengths include its high consumer recognition, an integrated and full-featured system, and connection to SoftBank Group synergies.

Weaknesses include depended on third parties for various activities, controlled company characteristics and some internal control weaknesses at newly-acquired subsidiaries.

Its opportunities are in line with national priorities for a transformation to cashless transactions and a shift from savings orientation to investment orientation. It is also integrating generative AI technologies wherever possible to reduce costs and accelerate product development.

The firm faces threats from all manner of financial services companies in each industry vertical it operates in, and is subject to the potential for changing regulatory guidelines or procedures, which may negatively impact its operations.

The company's Employee Benefits (how much it pays its personnel) as a percentage of total revenue have trended lower in recent reporting periods, a positive signal for cost efficiencies:

Employee Benefits

Expenses vs. Revenue

(Source - SEC)

Its Employee Benefits efficiency rate has also risen substantially as revenue has grown:

(Source - SEC)

PayPay’s Recent Financial Results

Revenue has grown impressively, accelerating its rate of growth in the most recent reporting period.

Operating profit has also increased materially, while cash flow from operations has grown dramatically, as the table shows below:

Total Revenue

(Glossary Of Terms)

(Source - SEC)

As of December 31, 2025, the company had $2.4 billion in non-customer cash and equivalents and $2.6 billion in borrowings.

Free cash flow for the trailing twelve-month period ending December 31, 2025, was an impressive $2.1 billion, with approximately $43 million in capital expenditures.

PayPay’s IPO Plan

PAYP filed a placeholder figure of $100 million for an IPO of American Depositary Shares representing underlying common stock, although the final figure will likely be significantly higher, potentially as high as $2 billion.

The company, shareholders and employees will be subject to the standard 180-day share lockup period.

PayPay said it intends to use the net proceeds from the IPO as follows:

We intend to use the net proceeds we receive from this offering for general corporate purposes, including working capital, sales and marketing activities, product development, general and administrative matters, and capital expenditures.

(Source - SEC)

Management said the company is not subject to any legal or administrative claims that would have a material adverse effect on its financial condition or operations.

Underwriters of the IPO include Goldman Sachs, J.P. Morgan, Mizuho, Morgan Stanley, Citigroup, Jefferies, BofA Securities, Societe Generale, Wolfe | Nomura Alliance, Cantor, Credit Agricole CIB, Daiwa Capital Markets, Deutsche Bank Securities, Natixis, SMBC Nikko, Barclays, ING, IMI - Intesa Sanpaolo, MUFG and Santander.

PayPay Is Growing Quickly And Profitably

PAYP has produced strong topline revenue growth, while recently becoming highly profitable after being at only operating breakeven at the end of fiscal 2024.

The firm has transitioned strongly from an investment phase to a very high free cash flow conversion to net income.

It has very low leverage relative to its EBITDA; net debt to EBITDA was just 0.3x in the nine months ended December 31, 2025.

Compared to US peers such as Block or PayPal, or Adyen, PAYP produces a much high net margin, although at a lower scale.

So, with a conservative balance sheet, very strong growth and profitability, and exceptional cash generation, the PAYP IPO will likely see strong institutional investor demand, despite a recent pullback by investors from fintech companies.

The firm is well-positioned for further expansion, either through strategic acquisitions or international initiatives.

PayPay is a foreign private issuer, meaning it can produce less information for shareholders at management’s option.

From the IPO filing, it looks like the company is disclosing quite a lot of historical information, so let’s hope that continues as a public entity.

The company benefits from its SoftBank+Yahoo Japan ecosystem integration, but is seeking to reduce its incentive intensity to improve profitability

With its investor syndicate including SoftBank, which includes affiliates Yahoo Japan and LINE, PayPay is positioned to be a leading player in the growing Japanese financial technology ecosystem that is challenging the established, legacy banking incumbents.

I’ll provide a final opinion when we learn more IPO details on pricing and valuation.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced

data.title: January CPI: Lower Inflation, But Not A Reason To Cut Rates
data.description:
The January CPI report offered an encouraging headline at first glance, with inflation easing to the lower end of its post 2021 to 2022 range. However, beneath that softer print was a more uneven mix of price dynamics, with weakness concentrated in volatile categories and firmer pressures persisting in select service segments. As a result, the data complicates the narrative of steady, broad-based disinflation and keeps the focus squarely on whether underlying price trends are being masked by softness in the major CPI segments.

CPI

The U.S. CPI increased just 0.2% MoM to start the new year, coming in below estimates of a 0.3% MoM increase. The soft monthly gain caused the annual rate to decelerate from 2.7% YoY in December to 2.4% YoY in January, a reading that was also below the consensus estimate of 2.5% YoY. This softer annual rate is the lowest since April 2025 and matches the second-lowest post-pandemic reading in September 2024. More generally, the movement in headline CPI inflation puts it back at the lowest part of the range that it has been in since the sharp flare-up in price growth in 2021-2022.

The softness was in large part driven by the volatile components, which were both weak in January:

  • Food prices increased just 0.2% MoM in January, much weaker than the 0.7% MoM increase in December. The food at home index increased at the same 0.2% MoM rate, the slowest for this subsegment since July 2025. Despite the weakness, 5/6 of the grocery store subsegments increased. Food away from home was up just 0.1% MoM, showing no major price pressures in January.

  • Energy prices were a major deflationary force in January, falling -1.5% MoM, despite there being exceptionally cold weather during the month. This was because prices for energy foods saw a sharp -3.3% MoM decline, driven by gas prices down -3.2% MoM and -7.5% YoY. Weather effects had more of an impact on the energy services segment, which increased 0.2% MoM, as prices for utility gas services were up 1.0% MoM. This specific subsegment had very little impact on the overall energy CPI movement due to its small contribution relative to gas prices.

The downside surprise in the headline CPI rate can be largely attributed to the soft moves in the volatile components. Not only was there a large decline in energy prices, but the momentum in food prices from the month before largely fell away.

Core CPI

The U.S. core CPI increased 0.3% MoM in January, meeting expectations of a 0.3% MoM increase, and the annual rate of 2.5% YoY also met expectations of a slight deceleration from the 2.6% YoY increase in December. Further disinflation in the core CPI puts the annual rate at its lowest in almost five years (lowest since March 2021). The moderate monthly gain in core prices was a result of some heat in services offsetting another cooler month in goods.

  • Core goods prices were unchanged for the second straight month in January, which helped to push down the YoY rate to 1.1%, the lowest since June 2025. Two segments stood out as especially weak in January: transportation goods (-0.7% MoM) and medical care goods (-0.1% MoM). The former was heavily affected by a -1.8% MoM decline in user cars and trucks, the largest decline in the sub-index since January 2024. Within the latter, prescription drug prices saw no MoM change and on an annual basis were down -0.5% YoY, the largest decline since October 2021. Weaknesses in these two areas helped to offset inflation in other segments.
  • Core services was the one hot segment in the January CPI report, rising 0.4% MoM in January, the largest single-month move since July 2025. This move can’t entirely be blamed on the shelter index, as it increased just 0.2% MoM. Instead, there were hotter moves elsewhere like transportation services (+1.4% MoM), other personal services (+1.6% MoM), recreation services (+0.4% MoM), and education & communication services (+0.4% MoM). This included some large moves in subindexes like airfare (+6.5% MoM), internet service providers (+1.8% MoM; largest ever), and both specialist healthcare services (+0.8% MoM) and hospital services (+1.0% MoM).

The January inflation report looks as cool as it does because of softness in major categories like energy and used vehicles, while other segments like services and food saw quiet monthly gains. When taking away these four segments, which admittedly do account for just under 60% of the entire CPI, prices were up 0.5% MoM in January, the largest increase since September 2022. So the story of today’s report is that while the headline rates did point to cooler inflation, there were many smaller segments pointing to pockets of remaining price pressures.

Based on which smaller segments saw these pressures, there is still not much evidence that tariffs are leading to persistent inflationary pressures. Segments known to be heavy with imported goods like apparel and household goods only saw moderate gains of 0.3% MoM in January, and both new vehicles (0.1% MoM) and vehicle parts (-0.5% MoM) were weak. More broadly, the short-term annualized 3Mo3M growth rate in core goods prices is just 0.4%, after peaking at around 2.5% in Q3 2025. Price pressures instead seem to be coming from consumer resiliency and wage growth that is holding up in a labor market that is showing more signs of tightening than loosening.

Market Reaction

The January CPI report initially suggested that inflation had eased meaningfully at the start of 2026, but a closer look at the underlying components painted a more nuanced picture. That contrast drove some volatility in the first hour of post-release trading. By the time markets settled, however, positioning reflected a modestly dovish tilt heading into Friday’s equity open.

  • S&P futures (SPX) ended up slightly higher in the first hour of trading after January CPI was released, but this led to a volatile open for the S&P 500 index (SP500), leaving it slightly lower by around 10 am EST. The clearest signal on investors’ feelings about inflation data is in the move in small caps. The rate-sensitive Russell 2000 (RTY) was up around 0.5%, outperforming the slight declines in the S&P 500 and NASDAQ.
  • The moves in the Treasury markets were also volatile, but in general, the yield curve is lower so far today. An initial decline in yields after the CPI release was reversed and then sustained in the hour after the data came out. Treasury yields with maturities of 2 years and longer are down anywhere from -3 bps to -4 bps, while short-term note rates are more or less even. The message from the market seems to be that short-term rate expectations are unchanged while longer-term rate expectations have shifted lower, possibly reflecting that there was still no evidence of persistent tariff inflation.
  • The moves in FX markets were also volatile, and the US dollar index ended up around even later in the morning. The index had firmed a bit yesterday, but soft inflation data presented an opportunity to reverse that move right after the CPI data was released, and that bias remained going into the NY market open.

Despite some dovish bias in trading across most financial markets, the federal funds futures market saw only a minor shift. The FedWatch probability of a pause in the March FOMC meeting (March 17th-18th) only fell -1.3 ppts to 90.3% after the January CPI data, meaning that the hawkish effect of the jobs report this morning on March rate expectations remains.

In short, while the latest inflation figures were modestly softer, they do not signal a shift from disinflation toward outright deflation. As a result, downside CPI surprises are unlikely to be interpreted as a catalyst for materially looser financial conditions, but rather as a check against further tightening. Near term rate cut expectations remain anchored primarily to labor market dynamics, which continue to show more resilience than deterioration based on the latest data. Against that backdrop, it is not surprising that the market response to softer CPI was comparatively muted relative to the reaction following the stronger jobs report earlier this week.

data.title: BlackRock TCP: Q4 Earnings Are Likely To Disappoint
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered BlackRock TCP Capital Corp (TCPC), I issued a sell rating due to the poor earnings and deteriorating portfolio quality. Since then, the share price has fallen ~17% and there are hints that the upcoming earnings may continue to disappoint shareholders. TCPC is set to report its Q4 earnings at the end of this month, so I wanted to revisit this business development company to share some thoughts around its outlook over the next twelve months.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that TCPC's share price has rapidly eroded capital and is now down more than 46.7%. Even when including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return still sits at a loss of about 37.5% over the same time frame. Due to the continuous decline in share price, TCPC now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 20.5%. However, I anticipate that the dividend will get reduced within the next twelve months as TCPC's portfolio continues to face headwinds.

Unfortunately, I do not believe there are any catalysts that can help propel TCPC higher in the short-term. In fact, I believe that management's most recent announcement about loan write-downs will actually drive the stock price lower and create additional selling pressure as investors shift capital out of TCPC. I anticipate TCPC to report much lower net investment income as well, which means that investors should still avoid accumulating here. So let's start by reviewing the biggest issue with TCPC's portfolio at this time.

Deteriorating Portfolio Quality

Management disclosed some new loan write-downs on their latest Form-8K. In plain English, this means that TCPC has admitted that several of the portfolio companies it lent money to are now failing. In order to generate its earnings, TCPC lends capital out to middle-market companies and collects interest and equity in exchange. Therefore, TCPC relies on a borrower's ability to keep up with their required debt maintenance. If a borrower fails to keep up with payments, they will get put into non-accrual status.

Non-accrual status means that the borrower cannot keep up with their loan repayment and aren't actively contributing towards TCPC's earnings. With non-accruals, there's always a chance that a borrower can work its way back to good standing once cash flows improve. However, these write-downs are much worse than non-accruals because they are essentially management admitting that there is zero chance of recovering the invested funds.

Therefore, the term 'write-down' actually means that management is writing down the value of this loan on their books to reflect reality: these positions are a loss. Management also confirmed that six investments made up two-thirds of the overall NAV decline. According to that Form-8K, the following companies presented the largest losses:

  • Edmentum
  • Razor
  • SellerX
  • HomeRenew/Renovo
  • Hylan
  • InMobi

Management stated that they have waived one-third of their management fee for the quarter to help offset these losses, but I am afraid that the damage is already done. So despite TCPC's prioritization of first lien senior secured loans and diverse portfolio, management's strategy has failed here. Therefore, I have little confidence that the upcoming earnings report will show any meaningful improvements.

TCPC is set to release its Q4 earnings on February 27th before market open. I believe that we will see a reduced NAV, no earnings growth, and a thinning dividend coverage. Seems like the current estimate is that TCPC will report net investment income of $0.27 per share. Considering the loan write-downs and the tone of its continuous NAV decline from the prior quarter, I think this is a fairly reasonable estimate. I think a net investment income anywhere between the range of $0.25 per share and $0.30 per share should be expected. A net investment income of $0.25 per share is the baseline since that is the current distribution amount.

Discounted Valuation For A Reason

At the time of my last coverage, TCPC traded at a large discount to NAV of 34.56%. Due to the continuous decline in share price since then, TCPC now trades at a larger discount to NAV of 43.51%. If we refer to the red line on the graph below, we can see how TCPC now trades at one of the largest discount to NAV price levels over the last decade, with the exception of the pandemic-driven decline in 2020. This massive discount to NAV pricing is a direct reflection of TCPC's current weakness and is a clear sign that investors should stay away from the BDC.

With the loan write-downs being an issue, I don't expect TCPC to report any positive growth momentum from new investments for Q4. Since interest rates remain elevated, I believe there is a solid chance that we will see another quarter of disappointing growth metrics, which includes sales and repayments outpacing the amount of capital that was allocated towards new investment activity. If this is the case, the NAV is likely to see more downside pressure over the next quarter since the portfolio has failed to offset losses with new investments.

I believe that higher rates may be one of the issues over the next twelve months. While the assumption is that interest rates will continue to trend downward, we don't really know how aggressive rate cuts will be. If the Fed chooses to continue moving forward with smaller 25 basis point cuts, this just isn't enough to really drive the needle forward for growth. A higher cost of debt will severely limit the number of businesses seeking debt capital, which means that TCPC will struggle to locate new growth opportunities.

A BDC's price to NAV level is usually a direct reflection of its performance and sentiment amongst investors. TCPC is currently trading at such a large discount to NAV valuation because it continues to erode investor capital over time. During this period of uncertainty, I believe it's always best to stick with the BDCs that have a long track record of success. I believe that two of the highest quality BDCs out there are:

  1. Ares Capital (ARCC)
  2. Main Street Capital (MAIN)

These are BDCs that consistently grow their NAV, produce positive net investment income, and support their distributions with ease. When running a performance comparison over the last three years, we can see how TCPC drastically underperforms these peers. Although ARCC and MAIN implement a different portfolio strategy and investment allocation than TCPC, I wanted to simply demonstrate that there are other choices out there. I see no reason for investors to remain on a sinking ship when there are alternatives that continue to navigate this challenging interest rate environment.

In order for TCPC to be upgraded to a hold rating, I would like to see sizeable portfolio growth and improved earnings going forward. Net investment income has continued to steadily decline from its high of $0.49 per share in Q3 of 2023. If net investment income were to increase back to its earlier range around $0.35 per share, this would be a great indicator that a turnaround may be taking place.

Secondly, if management were to achieve a shift to positive NAV momentum, this would be a sign that the dividend is supported and cash flows are improving. However, this requires TCPC to actively allocate significantly more capital towards new investment activity that can outpace the level of sales and repayments experienced. I would love to see several consecutive quarters where there is a positive net new investment activity amount that can offset the loan write-downs.

Dividend Cut Is Likely

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.25 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 20.5%. As I stated in my prior coverage, TCPC generated net investment income of $0.30 during Q3. When we take these loan write-downs into consideration, as well as a low level of new investment activity, I believe that there's a good chance TCPC will report net investment income that barely supports distributions. Like I said, I think it's very reasonable to expect TCPC's net investment income to land somewhere between $0.25 per share and $0.30 per share.

Even if net investment income still lands between this range, I believe it would be best for management to reduce the distributions going forward. A reduced distribution would lead to less pressure for TCPC to generate higher earnings and would leave excess income that can be used to strengthen its balance sheet. A reduced payout can also help slow the rate of TCPC's NAV decline over time. I would argue that a dividend cut would be interpreted as a good thing by investors, since it's a sign that management is trying to course-correct its portfolio.

If you are sticking with TCPC for the income that it provides, I believe there are less risky BDCs out there. This high dividend yield of 20.5% is not sustainable at the rate TCPC's portfolio is deteriorating. Furthermore, investors should consider the fact that the income received from TCPC is not tax-efficient. Therefore, TCPC would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that TCPC is still a sell at this time. There are no signs of conditions improving as we approach the Q4 earnings. Therefore, I believe that the net investment income will decline, alongside TCPC's NAV and portfolio quality. Management's recent announcement regarding the write-down of six loans confirms that the BDC's portfolio is struggling under this higher interest rate environment. Since there is no indication of how low interest rates will go throughout the year, I believe that TCPC's growth potential remains fairly limited and losses can widen. For these reasons, I believe that there is still downside risk and TCPC's large discount to NAV valuation should not be taken as an opportunity to accumulate. In order for TCPC to be upgraded to a hold, I would like to see improved net investment income, positive NAV momentum, and a greater level of capital invested into new opportunities so that the loan write-downs are offset.

data.title: Kraft Heinz: The Catalyst Is Gone
data.description:
Introduction

The Kraft Heinz Company (NASDAQ: KHC) saw its stock price decline by nearly 14% since I rated it a hold back in late April 2025. A lot has been going on at the company, such as the recent quarterly earnings and the suspension of its corporate split. With all of that in mind, I want to dive into why I believe that it is too little too late to justify an investment in the famed company.

Current Dynamics

KHC posted mixed earnings as Q4 Non-GAAP EPS came in at $0.67, a 6-cent beat, while revenue came in at $6.35B, a $20MM miss and a 3.5% Y/Y decline. For the full year, KHC reported net sales of $24.9B, also a 3.5% Y/Y decrease. Consolidated net income for the year was also highly overshadowed by the massive $9.3B non-cash impairment charge, primarily directed at the North American segment. This has pushed the company to report an operating loss of $4.7B for the year, and the impairment served as the formal admission that the carrying value of legacy assets such as Oscar Mayer had been overstated. The bottom-line beat was great, but it was largely due to disciplined financial oversight and top-line momentum or gross margin expansion.

Diving deeper, gross profit margins are sustaining pressure through 2025, ending Q4 at an adjusted rate of 33.1%. This has been mainly linked to inflationary pressures in commodity and manufacturing costs that successfully outpaced the company’s efficiency strategy. More specifically, the high input costs in the coffee category have required pricing actions that negatively impacted volume.

Segment-wise, we can assess that the North American category has been the epicenter of volume atrophy as it experienced a 5.4% decline in organic net sales during Q4. The full decline was driven by three areas of weakness that were identified by management: Lunchables, Spoonables, frozen meals, and snacks. For example, the erosion of Lunchables is highly significant as the brand was initially considered a resilient accelerator platform. The decline was attributed to a combination of headwinds, such as the reduction in SNAP benefits and a shift in consumer sentiment regarding ultra-processed foods.

On the international developed markets side, KHC saw a 2.4% decline in organic net sales during the quarter despite a pricing increase of 1.8 percentage points. This segment continues to face similar pressures to North America as consumers in European and Pacific markets grapple with cost-of-living increases and trade down to private labels. Though emerging markets were the singular bright spot in the portfolio as organic net sales grew 2.2% in the quarter, following a robust FY growth rate of 4.6%.

The quarter was further dampened by management’s decision to pause the previously announced corporate separation. I do not really agree with the move, as I believe that it could have unlocked underlying value for the two separate entities. The company’s internal review apparently stated that the organization was not yet healthy enough to undergo a complex corporate divorce. That was mainly due to the deteriorating industry conditions and financial dis-synergies. KHC expected to incur up to $300MM in dis-synergies, and by pausing the split, the company can avoid these costs and redirect capital toward its $600MM restoration plan. The stock initially declined nearly 7% on the release and picked up later on. I understand why, as the company lost its catalyst to unlock value, and thus I do not see how the company’s valuation could pick up in the near future. Plus, the $600MM reinvestment plan is a hard pivot that will carry high execution risks, as it remains unclear if a capital infusion can reverse years of brand erosion.

Despite the negatives, KHC still continues to be able to generate strong free cash flow, providing the funding mechanism for its ambitious reinvestment plan. For the full year, the company was able to generate $3.7B in FCF, representing a near 16% increase compared to 2024. This was the result of disciplined working capital management, focused on inventory. Furthermore, the company’s net debt/EBITDA ratio has stabilized at around 3x, while long-term debt stands at $21.2B, with $1.9B in senior notes maturing in June 2026.

It is also important to note that management has forecasted organic net sales to decline between 1.5% and 3.5%, including the headwind stemming from SNAP benefit reductions and a cautious view on consumer spending. EPS is set to range between $1.98 and $2.10, which would drastically be below the previous consensus estimate of around $2.49.

Valuation

The company could make the case for an undervaluation considering its underlying metrics, as it has a P/E Non-GAAP of 9.39, the lowest among its peers, as well as an EV/EBITDA of 8.27, also at the bottom of the range. I already mentioned that the company has a great FCF generation, and thanks to the low stock price, this translates into a high FCF yield of 12.6%. The firm also has one of the most attractive yields on the market as well, as it has a dividend yield of 6.58%. Many bulls would argue that KHC has a relatively strong moat in its brand equity, but that’s why I was bullish on a split, as it would help create a company that only has the best brands, but right now the portfolio is a mess of highly perceived brands such as Heinz and others that are seeing their volume erode away, such as Oscar Mayer and Ore-Ida.

There aren’t a lot of great growth prospects for the company in my view, except the $600MM restoration strategy gamble. It will aim to reverse years of identified underinvestment in the brand portfolio, such as in marketing spending, with a focus on product-focused creative for iconic brands. R&D spending will also increase by around 20% to 30%, with a key focus on renovating core products to meet modern needs, such as the launch of Power Mac, a version of Kraft Mac & Cheese with 17 grams of protein. $300MM will be dedicated to price, product, and packaging to improve competitiveness against private labels.

Risks

There is the current Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) overhang, as recent regulatory filings indicate that the conglomerate, which currently owns 27.5% of Kraft Heinz, may be looking to unload its 325MM shares. Warren Buffett already expressed dissatisfaction with the merger’s outcome in the past, and the withdrawal of such a high-profile and long-term investor would create a substantial supply of shares in the market, depressing prices further.

There is also a high risk with the $600MM investment, which might simply be too little, too late. Competitors like Mondelez (MDLZ) or even General Mills (GIS) have consistently outspent KHC on R&D and marketing over the past 10 years, and the rise of private labels has fundamentally changed the bargaining power of retailers like Kraft Heinz, as it stops them from maintaining premium prices.

Conclusion

Kraft Heinz is entering 2026 at a crossroads, especially as the last quarterly earnings confirmed the underlying weakness at the company’s core. As it scrapped its corporate split, I believe that the company does not have any positive catalyst that could force a re-rating of the stock. Despite its cheap valuation, I rate Kraft Heinz a sell.

data.title: Franklin BSP Realty Trust: A Dip Buying Opportunity As Portfolio Grows
data.description:
Thursday was certainly a wild ride with the significant market selloff, but rather than sowing panic I went shopping for stocks with major price dips to find value opportunities, and one sector I took a closer look at was mortgage REITs.

You may be familiar with the Franklin Templeton brand if you follow financial markets, but did you know they are also affiliated with an mREIT called Franklin BSP Realty Trust (FBRT)?

This REIT originates, acquires, and manages a portfolio of commercial real estate debt investments secured by properties located in the United States and internationally. In Thursday's selloff, I saw by afternoon that this stock was down around 15%, trading for just under $9 and its dividend yield pushing up around +14%, which certainly grabbed my attention.

As this is my first coverage of this stock, today's article took a holistic view across several fundamental topics and a few technical ones, in the wake of the firm's recent earnings results on Feb. 11th.

Thesis Summary: Bullish

My initial rating of this stock is a buy, which also happens to be the Wall St consensus as of Thursday. I would argue that it is an opportune time to snatch up the high dividend yield, also considering this firm's portfolio growth, high profit margin among peers, and low D/E ratio, with price upside forecasts being significant at this point.

Macro & Sector Outlook: Neutral

Putting aside short-term market dips for now, let's consider the larger sector of mortgage REITs. What you need to know about FBRT is that its primary focus is investing in "first mortgage loans," as they say on their own website, so this type of mREIT has a somewhat different risk profile than one whose primary focus is agency-backed MBS securities.

Considering its loan collateral exposure, as seen in its recent Q4 investor deck (pg 16), we can also see that the biggest loan collateral exposure for this REIT is multi-family residential:

I bring this up because for this strategy to work, the large majority of those multifamily properties have to be successful and continual cash flow engines, with very low default risk, and I think what could add more tailwind to that niche is growing housing demand plus little to no new supply. This could favor existing landlords. Regional population growth also can drive tailwind.

For instance, PwC wrote that "high-supply markets where weak rent growth will persist in 2026 and possibly into 2027 remain good long-term bets because of strong job and population gains."

From another angle, though, real estate firm CBRE had the following more cautious view:

Labor market challenges, coupled with persistent inflation and relatively high interest rates, have weakened consumer sentiment and will weigh negatively on household formation and new multifamily leasing activity in the first half of 2026.

So despite many considering multi-family residential a "defensive" niche, as opposed to office or retail, the potential headwinds certainly are there.

We can also see from NAREIT data that so far in 2026, residential was actually one of the underperformers among REIT sectors, with timberland being a clear leader, believe it or not.

Further, the same NAREIT study showed that so far in 2026, mortgage REITs slightly underperformed equity REITs as a niche:

So, my impression based on the multiple points above is to take a neutral view on this stock in this category. The reason I am not bearish here is that this mREIT has a diversified portfolio, despite having a large residential exposure, and is exposed to industrial and healthcare as well, two positive performers this year in the NAREIT data above.

Competitive Positioning: Highly Bullish

Next, let's consider a good peer comparable group for FBRT, and I think it could be the following 3 mREITs who are also primarily focused on first loans and not so much on agency-backed securities, with a wide range of market caps from $800MM to $7B: Blackstone-affiliated Blackstone Mortgage Trust (BXMT), Starwood Property Trust (STWD) who I recently covered and is the biggest of these by market cap, and Arbor Realty Trust (ABR).

Of these, FBRT is the smallest by market cap, but don't let size fool you, as peer data shows it had a 5-year revenue CAGR of nearly +21%, the best in this group, and also saw a return on equity/ROE of +5.8%, among the best two in this group.

Further impressive metrics show that FBRT can indeed compete in this niche, as momentum data shows its 5-year price return vs. the S&P 500 was the best in this peer group, at +33.5%.

Given the multiple strong points above and the association with a major financial brand like Franklin Templeton, I gave this stock a strong buy in this category, as I think its competitive positioning is impressive.

Company Growth Factors: Highly Bullish

When it comes to company growth in equity REITs, it usually is tied to growing future rental income, whereas with an mREIT like FBRT it would be growth of the loan portfolio, as that could drive future interest income.

Notably from this week's quarterly results, there is evidence of recent loan growth, and I think that is an upside. Consider what the company said in the press release (pg 1):

  • Closed $528.3 million of new loan commitments at a weighted average spread of 284 basis points.
  • Originated $1.1 billion of new loan commitments under programs with Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, and HUD.

Another growth factor is business acquisition, and in that regard, the company reported in the same press release (pg 2) that "on July 1, 2025, the Company acquired NewPoint Holdings," with the CEO Michael Comparato adding that "we have diversified our business lines with the NewPoint acquisition."

Additionally, Seeking Alpha data shows an expected fwd revenue growth of nearly +33% for FBRT, beating the peer group I created, and I think this could give added confidence to investors and analysts, despite the 15% dip in the share price which may have been largely due to the selloff that day.

Given the growth levers mentioned above, I am a strong buy in this category too.

Profitability Metrics: Bullish

Now, as we move to profitability metrics, key to know is that this isn't an equity REIT, so we won't be talking about FFO/AFFO, but I did a comparison of net profit margin vs peers:

I think this margin is most meaningful for impacting the EPS, a key headline metric for a mortgage REIT, and in this case with a margin of +17.1% it puts FBRT among the top two of this group, which is very impressive, and indicates some strong earnings efficiency over time.

If laser focusing more on the core operating margin, the firm is still impressive at over 71%, but from the chart below I want to point out some risk factors you'll notice, and that is the margin took a major dip during the pandemic years and also what seems to be the period of elevated interest rates, indicating this business is not immune to such market shocks:

What I do love about mortgage REITs, and I've invested in a few so far, is that they don't have the same property expenses as equity REITs since they don't typically own property but finance them, so the major cost is the cost of funding, and that can impact interest spreads. It is certainly prone to interest rate risk. The good news is that the funding costs seem to be declining, as the firm said in its Q4 investor deck (pg 8) that "average debt cost including financing was 6.5% in 4Q25 vs. 7.1% in 3Q25."

So, given that fact, and the impressive net profit margin, but also the risk of market shocks, I gave it a buy in this section rather than a strong buy.

Balance Sheet Risk: Bullish

The topic of balance sheet risk is just as relevant to mREITs even if their primary goal is not holding land and buildings, but loans. This can create asset risk exposure.

Earlier, I presented how the loan collateral is largely exposed to multi-family residential, and very little exposure to office or retail, so if considering multi-family a "defensive" posture then that could lower the asset risk exposure. However, some may argue that it is actually over-exposure to one asset class, and earlier I showed how this asset class has felt some headwinds lately.

Besides property collateral type, there is also geographic exposure, and from the q4 investor deck (pg 15) we see that FBRT has considerable geographic exposure across many regions and states of the US, and not just one, so that could also lower the risk lever:

Another metric tracked in this business is provision or benefit for credit losses, and notable from the Q4 results (pg 8) is that in FY25 this firm saw a benefit of $11.8MM vs the prior year's provision of $36MM, so it may indicate an improved loan loss outlook, a positive sign.

On the liability side, my go-to metric is the debt/equity ratio, and here is how it compares with peers:

With a D/E of 2.78, FBRT is the lowest of this peer group, so it indicates lower leverage in relation to equity, which I consider another positive for this firm.

With its former President, Michael Comparato, only recently named the new CEO, I'll be interested to see if the leadership strategy going forward continues to be one of lower leverage.

I am a buy in this section, and the evidence above supports it more than not.

Dividend Outlook: Neutral

My dividend view is threefold, focusing on yield, growth, and safety.

The yield touching 14% with Thursday's price dip may have already attracted high-yield investors, but the question is how long it will stay elevated and whether it drops if the share price recovers from the dip.

My bigger concern is that the payout ratio has regularly exceeded 100% recently, as income statement data shows, putting into question if EPS alone can cover dividends:

This could create greater dependence on raising cash via new debt issuance, to cover dividends.

As far as proven dividend growth, on a 5-year CAGR it has been flat, so this REIT has yet to prove a steady long-term dividend growth case.

So, given the high yield for now (which can change quickly), but also the high payout ratio and lack of proven growth, I am neutral in this section.

Valuation Metrics: Highly Bullish

The following is my worksheet which compares key valuation metrics to peers, and also shows how I determined a price target for this stock:

As this is based on data as of Thursday afternoon and the huge price dip that day, the argument here is clearly for a strong buy at this huge undervaluation, and if the market continues pulling the stock down for a few more days it could be an even better buy.

However, we are here for the long-term and not day trading necessarily. This price dip, if it lasts a little longer, presents an undervaluation opportunity on an mREIT with impressive profit margins and a large pool of multi-family residential loans rather than office or retail, but also a portfolio that is growing.

My price upside forecast implies +27% further upside to Thursday afternoon's price, so I am a strong buy here.

Technical Charting: Neutral

Looking past all the thorough fundamental topics today, to add another angle let's also consider technical chart patterns based on concepts like buy, sell, and hold signals using moving averages:

There has not been any classic buy signals (golden crosses) where the 50-day moves above the 200-day SMA, or sell signals (death crosses) where the 50 day moves below the 200-day, since the last one around April 2025. The two moving averages have been parallel for quite a while now, and Thursday's market action caused the share price to dip below the 200-day suddenly as you can see.

At this point, it seems this is a case of market-induced selling rather than a clear sell signal on this stock. In fact, with the two moving averages parallel, I am more inclined to be neutral here, and awaiting the 50-day breaking above the 200-day again.

While this moves the needle towards the middle, it did not materially lower the overall score which is still on the bullish side.

Risks and Challenge to Thesis

A risk to my bullish case could happen if Thursday's market scare is actually not a one-day or one-week event but rather an extended downturn, thereby making my outlook overly confident in the near-term anyway.

We should consider what drove the events on Thursday. CNBC wrote that "stocks dropped on Thursday as investors began to worry about the negative side of the artificial intelligence buildout, which threatens to disrupt the business models of whole industries and raise unemployment." It seemed also be driven by Cisco (CSCO) issuing weak guidance for the quarter.

This is actually where I think any REITs heavy into multi-family residential property actually should be considered as a defensive measure, since demand for housing is more of an essential need as opposed to sectors impacted by consumer spending confidence, or those that could be negatively impacted by AI. So, I think selling off REITs is unjustified due to jitters about AI.

Conclusion: Buy The Dip

To conclude, today I presented why this stock could be a strong dip-buying opportunity in the wake of this week's market selloff. Its key strengths moving the needle into the bullish column for me includes portfolio growth via new loans and business acquisition, a low D/E and impressive profit margin, and positive upside potential. Further, I've shown that this mREIT is both diversified as well as exposed to a niche considered more defensive, and its affiliation with a major financial brand like Franklin Templeton could further add weight to the upside view.

My call to action, therefore, is to add this mREIT to your watchlist of mREIT stocks to research further beyond just this article, in particular considering the various risk levers when it comes to mREITs like this one focused on loans vs. those focused more on agency MBS securities - and the vital role that both play in providing liquidity for the mortgage market.

data.title: 4 U.S., Canada REITs Announce Higher Dividends In January
data.description:
Three US and one Canadian real estate investment trusts announced dividend increases in January, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data.

Postal Realty Trust Inc. (PSTL), a specialty REIT focused on properties primarily leased to the United States Postal Service, announced a quarterly dividend of 24.50 cents per share, representing a 1.0% increase from its most recent dividend. This marks a 75% rise compared to the end of 2019.

Industrial REIT Modiv Industrial Inc. (MDV) declared a monthly dividend of 10 cents per share, reflecting a 2.6% increase from its most recent payout.

STAG Industrial Inc. (STAG) reported a quarterly dividend increase of 4%, bringing the dividend to 38.75 cents per share, an 8.4% increase since the end of 2019. The company shifted from monthly to quarterly dividends in January.

In Canada, First Capital REIT (FCR.UN:CA), which focuses on shopping centers, announced a monthly dividend increase of 2.5%, reaching 7.60 Canadian cents per share. This increase represents a 6.1% rise compared to the end of 2019.

Dividend increases by sector

In January, industrial REITs were responsible for two of the three dividend increase announcements, accounting for 16.7% of the sector. Combined with Postal Realty's announcement, those three increases represent 2% of public US REITs.

In Canada, the lone announcement represents 2.4% of the REIT market.

About this analysis

This Data Dispatch is updated monthly. The analysis includes publicly traded real estate investment trusts covered by S&P Global Market Intelligence that are based in the US or Canada and trade on the NYSE, Nasdaq, NYSE American, Toronto Stock Exchange or TSX Venture Exchange.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Weitz Ultra Short Government Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Portfolio Managers: Tom Carney, CFA® & Nolan Anderson

Investment Style: Ultra-Short-Term Bond

The Ultra Short Government Fund returned +0.96 in the fourth quarter compared to a +1.04% return for the ICE BofA US 6-Month Treasury Bill Index (6-Month Treasury). For calendar year 2025, the Fund returned +4.02% compared to +4.28% return for the index.

Overview

The Federal Reserve implemented three 25 basis point (bps) rate cuts in 2025 (two in the fourth quarter), bringing the federal funds rate down to a target range of 3.5% to 3.75% at year-end, primarily to support employment amid a weakening labor market. Future cuts faced uncertainty as the economy stabilized toward year-end and job growth showed signs of improving. The Fed signaled caution on further rate cuts, suggesting the pace would slow, with projections pointing towards fewer or paused cuts in early 2026 as they assess incoming data and economic stability.

The Federal Reserve’s monetary policy decisions (e.g., changes in short-term interest rates) will continue to affect all investments within our opportunity set. As a result, our yield and return will invariably follow the path dictated by the Federal Reserve’s monetary policy, as we frequently reinvest maturities with holdings that mature in a short period of time. As of December 31, approximately 98% of our portfolio was invested in U.S. Treasury bills and notes, a de minimis amount in investment-grade asset-backed securities, and a little more than 1% in a high-quality money market fund. The average effective duration was 0.3 years on December 31, unchanged from September 30. The Fund’s distribution yield was 3.73% as of December 31.

Under normal market conditions, the Fund will invest at least 80% of its net assets in obligations issued or guaranteed by the U.S. government and its government-related entities. The balance of Fund assets may be invested in U.S. investment-grade debt securities. Given current credit spread levels (very low), we will likely continue to be well above the 80% minimum level mentioned above.

Additionally, the Fund will maintain an average effective duration of one year or less. Duration is a measure of how sensitive the portfolio may be to changes in interest rates. All else equal, a lower-duration bond portfolio is less sensitive to changes in interest rates than a bond portfolio with a higher duration. Over time, this shorter-term focus (duration of less than one year) is intended to generate higher total returns than cash or money market funds, while also taking less interest rate risk than a bond portfolio with a higher duration.

The Fund’s principal investment strategies and objectives of providing current income, principal protection, and providing liquidity remain our primary goals. Thank you for your investment and confidence in our firm.

This material must be preceded or accompanied by a prospectus or summary prospectus.

All data is as of 12/31/2025 unless otherwise indicated.

30-Day SEC Yield represents net investment income earned by a fund over a 30-day period, expressed as an annual percentage rate based on the Fund's share price at the end of the 30-day period. Subsidized yield reflects fee waivers and/or expense reimbursements during the period. Without such fee waivers and/or expense reimbursements, if any; yields would have been lower. Unsubsidized yield does not adjust for any fee waivers and/or expense reimbursement in effect. Distribution yield is a measure of yield calculated by taking a fund's most recent income distribution payment divided by its net asset value (NAV) and expressed as an annual rate.

The opinions expressed are those of Weitz Investment Management and are not meant as investment advice or to predict or project the future performance of any investment product. The opinions are current through 1/20/2026, are subject to change at any time based on market and other current conditions, and no forecasts can be guaranteed. This commentary is being provided as a general source of information and is not intended as a recommendation to purchase, sell, or hold any specific security or to engage in any investment strategy. Investment decisions should always be made based on an investor's specific objectives, financial needs, risk tolerance and time horizon.

Data quoted is past performance and current performance may be lower or higher. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate, and shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Please visit weitzinvestments.com for the most recent month-end performance.

Investment results reflect applicable fees and expenses and assume all distributions are reinvested but do not reflect the deduction of taxes an investor would pay on distributions or share redemptions. Net and Gross Expense Ratios are as of the Fund's most recent prospectus. Certain Funds have entered into fee waiver and/or expense reimbursement arrangements with the Investment Advisor. In these cases, the Advisor has contractually agreed to waive a portion of the Advisor's fee and reimburse certain expenses (excluding taxes, interest, brokerage costs, acquired fund fees and expenses and extraordinary expenses) to limit the total annual fund operating expenses of the Class's average daily net assets through 07/31/2026.

The Gross Expense Ratio reflects the total annual operating expenses of the fund before any fee waivers or reimbursements. The Net Expense Ratio reflects the total annual operating expenses of the Fund after taking into account any such fee waiver and/or expense reimbursement, if any; total returns would have been lower had there been no waivers or reimbursements.

Effective 12/16/2016, the Ultra Short Government Fund revised its principal investment strategies. Prior to that date, the Fund operated as a “government money market fund” and maintained a stable net asset value of $1.00 per share. Performance prior to 12/16/2016 reflects the Fund's prior principal investment strategies and may not be indicative of future performance results.

Index performance is hypothetical and is shown for illustrative purposes only. You cannot invest directly in an index. The ICE BofA 6-Month Treasury Bill index is generally representative of the market for U.S. Treasury Bills. The Fund has also selected a broad-based index for regulatory requirements, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. This index is a broad-based index that measures the investment grade, U.S. dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market. See the Fund's Prospectus for additional information.

Holdings are subject to change and may not be representative of the Fund's current or future investments.

Definitions: Average effective duration provides a measure of a fund's interest-rate sensitivity. The longer a fund's duration, the more sensitive the fund is to shifts in interest rates. Basis Points (bps) refers to a unit of measurement that is equal to 1/100th of 1%, or 0.01%.

Consider these risks before investing: All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Market risk includes political, regulatory, economic, social and health risks (including the risks presented by the spread of infectious diseases). Changing interest rates may have sudden and unpredictable effects in the markets and on the Fund's investments. The Fund may purchase lower-rated and unrated fixed-income securities, which involve an increased possibility that the issuers of these may not be able to make payments of interest and principal. See the Fund's prospectus for a further discussion of risks.

Investors should consider carefully the investment objectives, risks, and charges and expenses of a fund before investing. This and other important information is contained in the prospectus and summary prospectus, which may be obtained at weitzinvestments.com or from a financial advisor. Please read the prospectus carefully before investing.

Weitz Securities, Inc. is the distributor of the Weitz Funds.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Northern Star Resources: A Quality Gold Miner Trading At Conservative Prices
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Northern Star Resources Limited (NESRF), I highlighted how they were trading below fair value despite strong financials, high-quality assets, and a robust growth pipeline as one of Australia's gold mining gems.

With gold (XAUUSD:CUR) reaching a new all-time high and retreating a bit in the meantime, as well as a very strong quarterly report despite a few issues at some of their projects, I believe NESRF continues to trade below their true potential in this environment, as the market seems to be accounting for quite conservative assumptions given the current price, which Northern Star could easily beat.

Internal Developments

As we could expect, Northern Star reported a massive increase in underlying net profit after tax, with a 49% jump in H1 FY26, despite selling only 729,000 ounces during the first half compared to 804,000 in H1 FY25.

However, despite this phenomenal performance, it’s important to note that the company’s realized prices were low compared to the spot prices at that time, with an average realized gold price of A$4,670 (US$3,292) per ounce compared to gold’s A$5,860 (US$4,135) average during Q4 2025, as a result of older contracts.

While their production was affected by crusher and mill issues at KCGM/Thunderbox, planned recovery work at Jundee and lower grades at Pogo, these lower prices came as the effect of delivering 158,000 ounces into their hedge book during the quarter, as the company ceased their hedge policy on July 2025, reducing their hedge commitments to 1.12 million ounces at an average price of A$3,333 (US$2,357).

On the one hand, this allows them to capture better spot prices going forward, but it also exposes them more to a potential fall in gold prices, with the company's CFO mentioning the following during their Q2 FY26 Earnings Call, as they want to focus on their current projects instead:

I think what I'd say is we want to be flexible to that [i.e. pre-delivering into the hedge book similar to peers]. It's probably unlikely. Any additional cash flow, we'll consider it. We've been thinking about it, but it's probably unlikely at this stage.

This is why I used to say before that gold prices aren’t the only thing that matters for a miner, as time at these high prices is just as important (in many cases) until these companies can really enjoy the maximum potential they come with, which is why I believe valuations should generally account for lower prices in general since that’s what these companies currently realize.

As a result of these issues, NESRF cut their guidance for FY26 to 1.6 million to 1.7 million ounces from 1.7 million to 1.85 million ounces, with an AISC between A$2,600 and A$2,800 per ounce (US$1,837 to US$1,979), which isn’t the best, but it’s also affected by the lower production, in line with their recent performance, but well above their previous guidance of A$2,300 to A$2,700.

Financially, we see these issues and the massive growth capital (which is up 34%, alongside a 61% increase in sustaining capital) lead to a very weak free cash flow, reporting a negative FCF of A$165.9 million or A$319.9 million during H1’FY26 when adjusting for the movement in bullion, payments for equipment financing and leases for operating assets, as well as maturation of term deposits.

As for their financial health, based on NESRF’s latest report, we continue to see a very strong position, with the current assets covering their current liabilities well and not a lot of long-term debt, with a solid amount of cash on hand available that can sustain their expansion and developments alongside the cash flow they produce.

Meanwhile, their semi-annual dividend remained unchanged, declaring A$0.25 for this half, in line with the dividend policy of returning 20% to 30% of their Cash Earnings, which is a good metric that gives us an idea about the company’s potential if they didn’t undergo the current expansion.

Macro Developments

By far the biggest development since publishing the previous article is the price of gold jumping to yet another record, briefly reaching ~US$5,600 before falling to a bit below $5,000 now, which is still a massive jump compared to the “high” ~$2,000 we saw half a decade ago.

Ultimately, this can be explained by the jump in demand, and even though the demand from central banks and other institutions dropped by ~37% in Q4 ’25 vs. Q4 ’24, investment demand more than offset this, with a ~73% increase in Q4 and 84% for the full year, with a massive 756% increase in gold ETFs and similar products as these rose in popularity and availability during 2025.

As we can see, jewelry has also been relatively resilient, only dropping by ~19% throughout the year despite the commodity’s jump, proving its inelastic demand, while the demand from technology was almost unphased.

Ultimately, this shouldn’t be that surprising, as I believe gold demand has been weak in recent years while the market ignored global fundamentals, focusing instead on AI, stay-at-home stocks, and others while only recently starting to realize it still needs to hedge against all this uncertainty, with what we saw recently being pent-up demand that built over the past years, basically continuing the pre-pandemic growth in a rapid burst now instead of a long-term increase.

This has been fueled by even more fear recently, as Japan’s economic plans and their national debt reaching a new record level put more pressure on the dollar, as the country is the largest foreign holder of US treasuries, and selling to boost their JPY and own bond market can have detrimental effects for the dollar and treasury yields, while European funds also reportedly sold some of their holdings following the Greenland situation, the new inflation data—which can be only the beginning of a wider trend that can be great for gold.

And all of this comes on top of an already weak and volatile environment, as the market still fears the potential AI “overspending” and overall weakness reported by many countries, including the U.S., where consumers are reportedly trading down even the basic products such as food.

Of course, this can also be attributed to all the pandemic money printing, crisis worries due to rising national debt, and the effects of more rate cuts on the dollar eventually being reflected in the price.

As for a company-level indicator, we can see the global AISC; the latest data (as of Q3 ’25) shows a continued increase in AISC, reaching over $1,500 per ounce recently and potentially sustaining a boost in production but also a potential safety net for these companies in the form of supply adjustments, as mentioned before.

Note, however, that the price is much more than just a factor of AISC, especially in such a “financially engineered” commodity, let’s say, as demand from various financial instruments (see the Investment section in the image above) ultimately makes the price. As a result, the actual supply is less relevant compared to commodities that are tied to “real” consumption and production, but that also makes gold’s potential nearly boundless.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, the company’s valuation will depend significantly on gold prices, and although I remain bullish on the commodity, there’s nothing that guarantees that the company will be able to enjoy high prices for long, with the timing of their one-off incidents being especially bad now.

Meanwhile, more incidents and potential delays can lead to the company missing out on even more of this upside, which can lead to them barely achieving anything throughout this massive jump in gold prices in case prices retreat.

Valuation

To be extra careful and take into account their weaker guidance and high CAPEX, I’ll assume $0 in free cash flow in FY26, but we should take into account projects such as the KCGM mill expansion (Fimiston Plant expected to commission in early FY2027) or Hemi (likely FY2030 at the earliest assuming a 2.5-year build period) advancing and supporting their production target of 2 million ounces in FY2029 once KCGM reaches a more stable state after their ramp-up and optimizations.

The cash earnings they report give a solid idea of the company’s potential once the growth CAPEX falls. Assuming a slightly better margin (thanks to high gold prices combined with the hedge naturally unwinding) than the ~A$1,330 per ounce if we annualize their H1'26 Cash Earnings to ~A$2.2 billion and use the midpoint 1.65 million ounces guidance, we should probably see about A$3 billion (US$2.12 billion) in free cash flow by FY29 once they reach and sustain their target.

I’ll assume they can grow to A$2 billion (US$1.42 billion) in FY27 following the initial ramp-up, growing to A$2.5 billion (US$1.77 billion) in FY28 and A$3 billion in FY29 (US$2.12 billion), assuming another conservative 25% increase thanks to Hemi’s ~0.55 million ounces of production and lower CAPEX contributing to their numbers from FY32 (to be extra careful and assume a slower ramp-up), minus some mine depletion at the other projects.

This would be followed by a 2.5% CAGR until FY35 and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, with a 10% discount rate to take into account their dependency on a volatile commodity but also high quality, with 1.43 billion shares outstanding as of their Q2 FY26 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $29.05 billion, for a price of US$20.31 per share, which is almost identical to the current US$20.75 despite the relatively conservative assumptions, meaning that the market is likely approaching this carefully as well, while better gold prices, the jump we should see soon thanks to KCGM and their hedge book being cleared (plus potentially better margins), can easily lead to the company beating expectations.

Conclusions

I reiterate Northern Star’s Buy rating, as the company continues to trade at a relatively conservative valuation despite their strong potential and solid financial health.

There are definitely risks to remember and potential to see lower prices, but the overall long-term potential and risk-reward level seem to be in the investment’s favor, albeit significantly smaller than before.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Natural Gas: Breakdown Below $3.2 Exposes Market To Deeper Slide
data.description:
By Parshwa Turakhiya

U.S. natural gas futures (NG1:COM) tumbled 2.55% to $3.14 per MMBtu, slipping decisively below the key $3.20 support zone and marking fresh multi-week lows. The decline underscores persistent bearish pressure driven by warm weather forecasts and ample supply, even as signs of rising international demand begin to emerge.

The move below $3.20 represents a significant technical breach, as that level had acted as a floor for much of 2025. With Spring approaching and heating demand fading, traders appear reluctant to rebuild long exposure.

Technical damage accelerates downside risk

From a chart perspective, the structure has deteriorated sharply. The Supertrend indicator, positioned near $4.64, remains firmly bearish, while the Parabolic SAR at $5.45 highlights the scale of the decline from January’s $7.5 spike. Prices are now trading well beneath all major resistance levels, reinforcing the strength of the prevailing downtrend.

The breakdown of the $3.2-3 zone shifts focus toward the $2.8–$3 region, levels not seen since 2024. Momentum remains negative, and the market has consistently rejected rallies above $5 throughout early 2025, establishing a pattern of lower highs. With no meaningful technical support until the upper $2.7 range, the path of least resistance remains lower unless a fundamental catalyst emerges.

The failure to hold the long-standing support band signals that speculative positioning tied to winter volatility has largely unwound, leaving sentiment fragile heading into the shoulder season.

International demand offers longer-term support

While domestic factors remain bearish, developments abroad may eventually provide a stabilizing influence. India’s Petronet LNG chief confirmed the country would purchase U.S. LNG at competitive prices, aligning with broader efforts to expand imports following recent tariff adjustments. India, currently the world’s fourth-largest LNG buyer, aims to raise natural gas’s share in its energy mix to 15% by 2030 from about 6%.

The country operates roughly 27,000 megawatts of gas-fired power capacity at only 25% utilization, constrained by high fuel costs. Lower LNG prices could unlock significant latent demand across power generation, fertilizers, refining, and city gas networks. Petronet is also advancing expansion plans, including development of a new east coast terminal.

Separately, ConocoPhillips (COP) announced a $2.1 billion investment to restart production at Norway’s Greater Ekofisk fields by late 2028, targeting 90 to 120 million barrels of oil equivalent in gas and condensate. Such long-term capital commitments suggest confidence in structural gas demand despite current price weakness.

As previously discussed, natural gas has struggled to sustain gains following its winter spike, with elevated volatility and heavy speculative flows amplifying moves in both directions. The latest breakdown confirms technical weakness, but expanding LNG demand in Asia and Europe may form a longer-term floor once domestic oversupply stabilizes.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Birkenstock: Still A Long-Term Hold, But Not A Perfect Story (Downgrade)
data.description:
A startling rotation is taking place in the stock market in 2026. Investors are still chasing after red-hot semiconductor stocks, whose products are in short supply as companies continue the datacenter buildout boom. And yet there is backlash against tech and growth stocks in general, over fears that AI can unseat software giants and be a net headwind rather than a tailwind.

Against this backdrop, traditional consumer companies that are in sectors that sold off last year are well positioned for a rebound. Birkenstock Holding plc (BIRK), the German shoemaker, looks potentially appealing after a sharp slide over the past year. Expectations for growth have indeed settled lower for the company as Birkenstock fights the challenges of its own scale. Shares have lost ~30% over the past year, and a decent fiscal Q1 earnings print did little to spark enthusiasm in the name again.

I last wrote a Strong Buy article on Birkenstock in December, when the stock was trading higher at $42 per share. Since then, Birkenstock has shed about 10% of its value. While cheap, I do think more risks are emerging for the company. Margins are eroding due to both tariffs and a stronger euro. Sales in developed markets are slowing as Birkenstock fights saturation, even though sales in China remain robust. All in all, while in the long haul I still see Birkenstock in a positive light and am certain to have carrying value, I also think this is a stock that requires patience. In the abundance of value alternatives (especially amid the sharp selloff of growth stocks), Birkenstock’s own value orientation amid execution challenges is no longer a huge unique draw, and I’m resetting my rating on the stock to a Buy rating (moderated down from my previous Strong Buy). To me, this stock has become a show-me story.

To me, there are now arguments on both the bull and bear sides of the coin for Birkenstock. On the positive side for the company:

  • Growth at scale and deeper penetration into new markets. Despite being at nearly a ~$2 billion annualized revenue scale and being a brand that has existed for hundreds of years, Birkenstock still manages double-digit revenue growth, which hopefully tailwinds in Asia, which is growing at a >30% y/y clip.
  • Relatively rich margin profile. Putting aside FX and tariff headwinds, we like the fact that Birkenstock carries a high 50s gross margin. Higher full-price sales and recent investments into manufacturing capacity and efficiency have allowed Birkenstock to maintain margins that sit above most consumer apparel peers.

But while I remain bullish on the company, there are also emerging risks to keep in mind:

  • Competition is aplenty in a market where consumers are constantly chasing what’s new. Growth deceleration in Birkenstock’s most mature markets, the U.S. and Europe, does showcase the brand’s age. Fashion trends change quickly, and Birkenstock’s recent inability to keep up with street growth expectations reflects the company’s challenges in continuing at its current pace of growth.
  • Rising leverage. Though Birkenstock’s debt is certainly nowhere near “red flag” levels, recent investments into production capacity and waning gross margins have boosted debt levels, now sitting at a 1.7x leverage ratio against trailing EBITDA (versus 1.5x last quarter).

Ultimately, it's a cheap valuation that keeps me invested in this stock. As shown in the chart below, Birkenstock trades at just shy of a 10x forward adjusted EBITDA multiple:

All in all, my advice on this stock is bifurcated. If your horizon is long-term and you don’t mind near-term volatility, I consider Birkenstock to be a safe brand, especially at moderate multiples - hence my more moderate buy rating. That said, those looking for nearer-term rebounds would do better bottom-fishing in the software sector for some true deals.

Q1 Recap

Let’s now go through Birkenstock’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The fiscal Q1 (December quarter) earnings summary is shown below:

Birkenstock's revenue grew 11% y/y to €401.9 million, which slightly missed Wall Street's expectations of €402.9 million. Now, it's fair to say that the strengthening of the euro, particularly against the dollar, has weighed heavily on Birkenstock. Constant currency growth in the quarter would have been much stronger at 18% y/y. That said, even this metric decelerated two points versus 20% y/y growth in Q4.

The chart below, meanwhile, showcases Birkenstock's sales growth by channel and by region.

There are positives and negatives to weigh here. On the one hand, we appreciate the fact that Birkenstock continues to achieve blazing growth rates in Asia, where constant currency growth of 37% y/y accelerated three points relative to 34% growth in Q4. Needless to say, the Asia region is a massive and relatively greenfield territory for the company: and with Asia still representing barely 15% of sales, the strong traction here is a clear anchor for Birkenstock's growth priorities. Europe, Birkenstock's second-largest market, also accelerated slightly to 17% y/y growth, from 14% constant-currency growth in Q4.

That said, mature markets wobbled. Constant currency growth in the Americas, which is more than half of the company's sales, slowed four points sequentially to 14% y/y. There are a plethora of shoe brands to choose from in the U.S., and the macroeconomic overhang and slowdown in consumer spending don't help either.

We're also nervous about the fact that B2B sales growth of 24% y/y far outpaced 12% DTC sales growth. The company made no comments about sell-through or channel inventory, so a part of the quarter's sales "strength" could be due to the timing of channel orders. If sell-through doesn't keep up, future restocking orders could be down.

Speaking to the company's growth priorities on the Q1 earnings call, CEO Oliver Reichert noted as follows:

In EMEA, our highest margin segment, markets like Germany, Denmark and Austria have reached penetration levels similar to the U.S. and still generate double-digit growth. But we are underpenetrated in other markets like France, Spain, U.K. and the GCC. So we see even stronger growth potential in these countries and very high margins.

Finally, the largest opportunity for long-term growth remains in APAC countries such as China, Japan, South Korea and India, where we are highly underpenetrated, but realize strong margins and some of our highest ASPs. We will steer APAC growth at double the pace of the other segments over the next 3 years. This means we will double our APAC revenue by 2028. For the foreseeable future, we expect B2B growth will continue to outpace DTC growth. But we are working to balance channel growth and strengthen our DTC business. B2B growth is driven by the trend towards in-person shopping. We are investing in our own retail to capture more of this in-person demand and promote newness. In online, which accounted for 80% of our DTC revenue last year, we are not sitting on our hands. We are transforming our capabilities to convert more of the lifetime value of the brand fan to our e-com business. We do this all within the context of our vertically integrated supply chain and manufacturing capabilities. Our supply chain will deliver the unit growth required to achieve our 3-year targets."

My second concern is on margins. Adjusted EBITDA pulled back to a 26.5% margin in Q1, falling -170 bps y/y. Nominally, adjusted EBITDA still grew at a 4% y/y pace to €106 million.

The good news is that most of the headwinds are outside of Birkenstock's control. Without the 230 bps drag from the strengthening of the Euro, Birkenstock's gross and adjusted EBITDA margins would have risen slightly. That said, the company's price increases and manufacturing efficiencies were offset by U.S. tariffs.

Key Takeaways

Looking longer term, Birkenstock still has substantial opportunity to expand in Asia, as well as large markets in Europe outside of Germany. The company's Pasewalk capacity expansions continue to be boosting the company's product margins, though tariffs and FX are eating into the company's profitability.

All in all, Birkenstock remains a bargain at <10x forward adjusted EBITDA. That said, given a risk-off sentiment in the markets at the moment, I wouldn't bank on a fierce near-term rebound here.
